speaker: interviewer
text: I am Donna Canter, the interviewer. Today's date is March 16, 1995, and this is tape two. Let's pick up with the haystacks.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was our first hiding place, my brother's and mine. But after a day, my brother went out, because he was much, much older than me. And he tried to find where the transport was and where they were working, so he could sort of blend in. But he came back for me. And he told me that this is not a good place to hide because they already found a few people and killed them. So we-- we found another place, and it was inside a building. In a wall, there was a-- an opening that was no bigger that my body could fit through, which I was eight years old-- could fit squeezing in. That hole was this big that I had to squish in, squish in like my shoulders, to get in there. And four people were hidden in there. We just had enough place to-- to just be practically one on top of another, squished, sitting, or-- or-- or almost like just barely sitting. I don't know how long we were in there, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think we could stay there too long because our bodies were-- we just couldn't take that long of a time to be in those positions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When-- when we came out of there, we found another place in between-- between big [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tubes, like it was like-- probably was the-- the boiling place for the bricks, or whatever it was. It was pipes, big pipes. Must have been. And the opening looked like a kettle opening for like a giant oven- type kettle. And inside were pipes. And we sat on those pipes for a long time. I think this was the longest place we were hiding. And until one day, we had to get out. It took two months to resettle the camp. I found that out. So I guess for two months, we were hiding from place to place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in-- in-- when we were in that, amongst these pipes, when I got out, my feet were all swollen because from the cold of the pipes. And I could hardly move. And we found another place, which was warm in a building. And, somehow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother went out, and I guess he found work, and I was all by myself. And one day, I just-- I couldn't take it. And I tried to go find someplace else where there are some people. I-- I went out, and I went over to where they were working, and they were working-- some of them were working to plant-- plant things that we, in turn, I guess, later on, I found out, where we would eat-- eat those-- like, vegetables, or whatever they were planting, and it must have been early spring, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were planting. So that's the only deduction I can make. And I tried to work. And I remember, I've seen some people from our camp, I've seen even the Germans. And nobody tried to stop me. He asked me how old, and I said I want to work. And somehow, he didn't stop me. He didn't-- he didn't turn me back, he didn't do anything. But, pretty soon, somebody approached me and told me, I better get out of here. It could have been my brother. It could have been my little brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To get out of here because they're going to take me away if I don't get out of here. And I made myself disappear. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I-- I-- I went, and I-- I tried to find another hiding place. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] While I was looking for that other-- finding the other place, a Ukrainian soldier, who was like a German-- in those days, you know, they were all even worse than the Germans-- he stopped me. And I knew my goose was cooked. I-- I didn't have a chance. Because he stopped me, and-- and it must have been at the end of the day. And he took me over to where a group of women formed to go off to the camp. And he-- he brought me there, and he identified himself to-- to the German SS [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was leading-- leading that group. And at that group, there was also the head of our camp was there, the Jewish head of our camp was there. And there were a lot of other people. But the group itself was all women. And while he was identifying himself to the SS and telling him about me, my mother saw me in the group. And she pulled me into the group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And where the SS man asked him where-- he was looking around for me, because he had told him something or another. I wasn't there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this time, the SS had a big poke in his hand. And he went amongst the women, and he just went with the poke, and he hit everybody on the side, on each side, until he got to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was holding onto my hand. And she had like some food in her arm, like a bag of something. He took the bag, and he threw it, took it out of her hands. He threw it, and he hit her very strongly with that poke, and he grabbed my arm. And by that time, the whole group sort of separated, because they were scared to death of him. And he threw me against the wall. I don't know how far that wall was, but it was very far away, especially for me, as a kid. Because, the next thing I knew, I must have passed out, because when I-- when I came to, there was nobody there. I was all by myself with that SS. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He got me to stand up. And he-- and-- and by that time, these Polish kids were standing around and-- and laughing. And he was asking me whether I knew where people were hidden. And I-- I said, no, I don't know. So he-- he took me around to a couple places where I know people were there or used to be there, and he couldn't find anybody. So I tried to make a deal with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, look, if you don't find anybody, can I go and see my mother and father the last time? Because I knew what he-- he had in mind. And-- and those Polish kids were following us around wherever we went. And it seemed like an eternity because we went through that whole place, and in my child's eye, it was a very large place, many buildings, and many places to go and see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They even took me to that kettle where-- where I sat amongst the pipes. And there was a couple of corpses there inside. And he couldn't find anybody. So he brought me back to that wall, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he kept on choking me on the way. I had a scarf on, and he put it around my neck, and I put my chin in, so-- so that he won't choke me. And I said, you're going to-- you promised you're going to let me go if you don't find anybody. And he was so angry. His anger was unbelievable. I mean, he just was-- whether he had a quota to perform or something, because he was so angry at me. And those kids-- those-- there were at least two, or three, or four Polish kids standing around saying, she knows where they're hidden. And they said it in Polish. I can't recall anymore how to say it, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at home, we never spoke Polish, we spoke Yiddish. I only learned Polish sort of by osmosis, or listening to all around me, over the years, as I was a child. Finally, he took me back to that wall, and he told me to turn around. And he had his revolver out. He took it out, and he was pointing it at me at a fairly-- well, it was not very close range, but close enough range. And he was going to shoot me. He said turn around, face the wall. And-- and I kept saying, but you promised you're going to take me back and see my mother and father one more time. I was very strong-minded, but he wouldn't let go. And at that moment, when he pulled this revolver, another SS was passing by. He was as drunk as can be. And he was-- I couldn't see him until I turned around.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was talking to him while I was still turned around. And he told him, in-- in other words, are you going to waste a bullet? She's going to be killed, anyhow. And I understood because-- because German was so close to Yiddish. And at that time, I heard enough German already to know quite a few words in German, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that SS, he was so angry at himself, and he put down, I guess, his revolver, and he told to the century that was at that gate there to take me back to the camp. And this is how I became-- I came into the camp, into the concentration camp of Ostrowiec. It was called [NON-ENGLISH], which means a hard labor camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I became one of the workforce in that camp. Naturally, I did not stay-- men and women were separate, and I did not stay with my family. I stayed with my mother, but during the day, we were working. We used to march off, and, somehow, at that particular time, there was a-- they took the children to where we were working in a Hitler-Jugend [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] camp was in Ostrowiec, where the Hitler-Jugend was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and it was like, probably like a country club, as you would call it here. They had tennis courts. They had a club room. They had a theater, and they had soccer fields. It was like a whole big, complex, and we were in charge. And there were children, and we were in charge of keeping it in order. And I remember doing single-handedly, pulling one of those big-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Polish, it's called the [POLISH]. I don't know how to say it in English. It's like a big, heavy duty-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Heavy duty-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pull, that you pulled. And it was made out of iron, so that it can-- it can flatten-- it was flattened the tennis courts. It must have been clay. The tennis court must have been clay, because we had to flatten the earth, that clay, that red clay with that [POLISH], which is a huge-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Steam roller?

speaker: interviewee
text: Steam roller, that's it. A huge steam roller. And I had to pull it all by myself. Now, once you got it started, it wasn't so bad. But it was just almost impossible to get it started. And I remember, in those-- at that time, I-- I must have been very, very emotional because I would cry at anything. And I remember trying to pull it and cry. And pulling it and pulling it. And when we were in-- when we would end our work at the end of the day, we would go into that club room where we gathered, and they would march us back to the camp, to-- back to the camp. And while we were crossing that part, they had the-- when the Hitler-Jugend, when they were there, their favorite sport was to have-- to have dogs. And they would let those dogs go on us and see, between crossing that street, how many can catch us and-- and bite us. I mean, it was pretty hair-raising to try to beat those dogs. But, thank goodness, they weren't always there. And if they were there, there was only a few at a time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, during that time, we had to clean-- we had to clean and make sure there were no weeds next to the soccer fields. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember particularly having to clean that theater. I-- I have a particular memory of given like a small bucket and something in my hand to wash that floor. And-- and-- and me looking back, and that place looked so big, whether it was the stage, or the downstairs. And it looked like endless. And the tears were just rolling down my eyes, with that little rag. And I was afraid they're going to punish me if I don't get it done. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, it-- it was very, very, very emotional, and I remember just-- stream-- my tears were just streaming down my face, and I was doing my work, and-- and I couldn't stop crying and looking back, and that place seemed to look bigger and bigger all the time. And I was afraid I'll never get it done. I don't remember whether I did or not. I don't. I just remember that awful, terrible feeling and that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] helplessness, helplessness, sitting there on the floor on my knees, and-- and that vastness of that place when I looked back. It wasn't hard work. It was nothing. It was just the overwhelming feeling of the vastness of it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is mainly what we did in the camp. And every once in a while, they would also take us to the factory to do some more loading of bricks. But mostly, we did take care of that Hitler-Jugend camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the line, trying to escape, and your mother pulled you into a line, you said that you knew they were being taken to a concentration camp. How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were working. They were working. They were adults. I assumed. I assumed because they were adults that that's OK. And the people that saw me there, you know, they weren't very happy to see a child because it means that their-- their life was jeopardized. I was not very welcomed either way because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] amongst-- amongst the people in the camp, children were not welcome because, first of all, a lot of people were angry their children were not there. A lot of people were angry because I jeopardized their lives. And a lot of people maybe would have liked to have helped me. But most of them, I-- I presented a terrible threat, terrible threat being there, next to them, at any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I wasn't very welcomed by-- by my own people either at that-- in those years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very difficult [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to survive as a child because I didn't know anything. The only thing I knew is that if my mother is there, my father is there, that must be OK. Then I'm safe. Then I'm OK. Because my brothers are there, my father is there. Then it must be fine. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to tell my mother not to-- when she pulled me in, I said no, he knows, he knows, he knows I'm here. But I guess she didn't hear me, or-- or-- it happened so fast. I mean, this incident happened so fast. I'm telling it slowly, but it happened very fast. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was just amongst the few times that I came face to face [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with-- with death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the only thing I could think of is wanting to see my parents. Nothing else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first time I saw a dead person was my grandfather. He-- he had a heart attack when the first bomb came on Lublin. I think it came on Lublin, if I'm not mistaken, I was told. And he came. He was there to visit my brother in-- in the yeshiva. And he also had family there, too. His own immediate family lived in Lublin. And I think the first bomb, he had a heart attack. And when he came home, he died. And I remember his body laying out to rest on the floor. And, in those days, things were different when people died. And this is the first time I saw a dead person, when my grandfather was laid out in his own house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I was-- when the second selection came-- well, the first and the second time I saw them is when they were dancing on their robes in that square. By that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a daily occurrence after that. It was a-- a daily occurrence. They were always-- there was always death somewhere. And I don't know, I seemed to overlook everything. I-- I must have placed myself in a completely different place. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was, most of the time, very jolly as a child when I was around my parents, and I used to sing. And I think maybe that's what helped me survive. I used to absorb songs, and I used to be so keen, and I wanted to learn. There was very seldom anything that passed by me that I didn't absorb, especially if it had to do with singing or-- or songs or verses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think that's how I preoccupied myself most of the time in those days. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That-- that camp was-- there-- they-- they built another little camp after that, and so they split us sort of up to go to different places to work. So it was a small camp. I think there were like two barracks in the other camp, maybe. And I used to be able to go there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where the men-- a lot of-- mostly men were in that camp because they were working. And I remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] going-- going there with the groups, naturally. You didn't go by yourself anywhere. But since I was accepted in the camp, I was part of the group. And I used to be able to sneak in, and I felt very confident. I-- I-- I guess maybe I wasn't scared of that part. Because I remember, I snuck into that camp, and my father was in that camp. And next to him was an old man, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he taught me that song that-- he taught me a song that-- that I have-- I have performed quite a few times. I did-- I-- I sang it in Auschwitz. I sang it at Yom HaShoah in-- in-- in-- in the  DP camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also did it in 1989, I think, at Yom HaShoah here, in Los Angeles, which was a very hard thing for me to recollect it, because I-- I hadn't-- I hadn't written it down, and it's a monologue that lasts approximately four minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it is not a little song. It's a big story. And this is where I learned it. I learned it from that old man in that other camp in Ostrowiec, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where there were mainly men there. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I really don't know time. I know we were busy all the time. Our food, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember, they were cooking for us in that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was surrounded by ditches. And when the ditches filled up, there was mud and water in the ditches. I guess that was for safety's sake, for if anybody tried to escape. There were no electrical fences, but the ditches. And they contained [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] frogs. And they used to catch the frogs and cook them. And we had frog soup. I mean, we ate pretty well in that camp. I even remember helping out in the kitchen in that camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Naturally, there were always selections, and there were always shootings, and people trying to escape, and being caught, and brought back. And we always had to watch. We always had to watch everything. I mean, that was part-- part of the rituals, that everybody had to attend. And-- and there was always the German there, probably telling us, this is your fate. And I slept with my mother. And I still wet. I used to wet every night, every night. And we were-- we were laying on boards. And I don't know if it was hay on it or whatever, but it didn't make a difference. I was young, and it didn't bother me either way. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you place this-- in what time period? Before the Hitler-Jugend?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was all in one era. This was all in one era. We were in that camp for at least a year and a half. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because total time that I was in the ghettos and in the camps was 3 and 1/2 years. So I would place that year and a half in that camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was working most of the time while I was there. And so when I got to Auschwitz after that camp, I was 10 years old. So between the ghettos and Auschwitz, the two years elapsed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you worked for the Hitler-Jugend--

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: -- In that camp, was that at the-- simultaneous?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when I was in Ostrowiec [INAUDIBLE]. That was during that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went into the line with your mother, where she grabbed you--

speaker: interviewee
text: After. This is all after. I was legit. When they took us to work, I was legit. I was a legit [GERMAN], as they used to call us. It means prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's pick up with your deportation with the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
